coredata.dc:title: Construction of multicolor solid state fluorescence carbonized polymer dots from pyromellitic dianhydride by HCCC method and their multiple applications
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Pengyi
Song, Jinping
Li, Shutao
Guo, Xiaoting
Xue, Jianhua
Ma, Qi
coredata.dc:description: In this research, four CPDs with different solid-state fluorescence were successfully synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal condensation cross-linking carbonization (HCCC) approach employing pyromellitic dianhydride (PMDA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as the polymerization monomers. It was proposed that there are two competing reaction pathways in the formation stage of polymers, and regulation of reaction conditions can affect this competitive relationship to acquire CPDs with different solid-state fluorescence. The morphology and surface structure of four kinds of CPDs were characterized in detail using several characterization techniques. These four CPDs not only possessed small particle sizes of 1.83–2.36 nm, but also exhibited excellent fluorescence stability. Results displayed that CPDs-1, CPDs-2, CPDs-3, and CPDs-4 solid powders exhibited fluorescent yellow, yellow, brownish-yellow, and brown fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp, which could fabricate fluorescent films and apply in LED devices. Interestingly, it was noticed that the modulation of the content of CPDs in CPDs/PVA composite films or the surface of LED chips could greatly adjust the color of composite film and LED light emission. In sensing studies, CPDs-2 could serve as fluorescence probe to construct a sensitive and selective sensor for the detection of luteolin, the linear range was 0–100 µM and the detection limit was 30 nmol/L. In practical sample analysis, the recovery rate and the RSD were 94.96 %–107 % and 0.54 %–3.52 %, respectively. In addition, CPDs-3 was used to construct a fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of water in the range of 0 % to 90 %. Moreover, as a potential nano-fluorescent probe, CPDs-2 showed low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility in cancer cell imaging research and plant seed growth processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Multicolor emitting
Solid-state fluorescence
Fluorescence sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PVA fluorescence film and construction of LED chip
Fluorescence assay of luteolin based on CPDs-2 probe
Determination of luteolin in practical samples
Sensing of water content in DMSO
Preparation and characterization of multicolor solid-state CPDs
Optical properties and stability of CPDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS full spectra of CPDs and the corresponding high-resolution C1s and N1s spectra.
(A) TEM images of CPDs and the particle size distribution histograms, (B) XRD spectra of CPDs, (C) FTIR spectra of CPDs, (D) H-NMR spectra of CPDs, (E) zeta potential histogram of CPDs.

(A) Optical photographs of CPDs in different solvents under sunlight and UV light, (B) Optical photographs of CPDs powder under sunlight and UV light, (C) UV–vis adsorption spectra, excitation and emission spectra of CPDs-1, CPDs-2, CPDs-3 and CPDs-4 aqueous solution, (D) Fluorescent films and LED devices based on CPDs.

(A) Fluorescence intensity ratio of CPDs-2 in the presence of different targets (1–28: blank, Al3+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, NO2 −, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, NO3 −, Cl−, I−, Br−, Ac−, S2−, rutin, genistein, quercetin, luteolin), (B) Fluorescence spectra of CPDs-2 upon the addition of various concentrations of luteolin (from top to bottom: 0–100 μM), (C) plot of (F0-F) versus the concentration of luteolin, (D) lifetime decay of CPDs-2 aqueous solution, (E) lifetime decay of CPDs-2 aqueous solution after adding luteolin, (F) The Stern-Volmer curves at different temperature. (G) Fluorescence spectra and optical photograph of CPDs-3 containing different content of H2O, (H) CIE pictures of CPDs-3 with different water content, (I) Linear fitting equations between F0-F and water content.

(A) Cell viability of MCF-7 after incubation with diverse concentrations of CPDs-2 for 24 h. (B) Photographs of black bean seedlings after cultivating black bean seeds by CPDs-2 solution with different concentrations (from left to right: 0, 300, and 500 mg/L) for 7 days. (C) Photographs of Mung bean seedlings after cultivating Mung seeds by CPDs-2 solution with different concentrations (from left to right: 0, 300, and 500 mg/L) for 7 days. (D) LSM images of MCF-7 after cultivating with 300 μg/mL of CPDs-2 solution for 1 h. (E) LSM images of transverse sections of black bean seedling stem after cultivating with 500 μg/mL of CPDs-2 solution. (F) LSM images of transverse black bean seedling stem sections after cultivating with distilled water.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure of fluorescent CPDs and its potential applications.
Determination of luteolin in practical samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Construction of multicolor solid state fluorescence carbonized polymer dots from pyromellitic dianhydride by HCCC method and their multiple applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Shanxi Datong University, Datong, Shanxi 037009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Shanxi Datong University, Datong, Shanxi 037009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Xinzhou Normal University, Xinzhou, Shanxi 034000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Xinzhou Normal University, Xinzhou, Shanxi 034000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Shanxi Datong University, Datong, Shanxi 037009, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Four carbonized polymer dots with different solid-state fluorescence were prepared using HCCC strategy. Two kinds of competitive condensation pathways were involved in the polymer formation stage. They possess versatile application potentials in fluorescence sensing, the construction of fluorescence composite films and LED devices, and fluorescence bioimaging.

In this research, four CPDs with different solid-state fluorescence were successfully synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal condensation cross-linking carbonization (HCCC) approach employing pyromellitic dianhydride (PMDA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as the polymerization monomers. It was proposed that there are two competing reaction pathways in the formation stage of polymers, and regulation of reaction conditions can affect this competitive relationship to acquire CPDs with different solid-state fluorescence. The morphology and surface structure of four kinds of CPDs were characterized in detail using several characterization techniques. These four CPDs not only possessed small particle sizes of 1.83–2.36 nm, but also exhibited excellent fluorescence stability. Results displayed that CPDs-1, CPDs-2, CPDs-3, and CPDs-4 solid powders exhibited fluorescent yellow, yellow, brownish-yellow, and brown fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp, which could fabricate fluorescent films and apply in LED devices. Interestingly, it was noticed that the modulation of the content of CPDs in CPDs/PVA composite films or the surface of LED chips could greatly adjust the color of composite film and LED light emission. In sensing studies, CPDs-2 could serve as fluorescence probe to construct a sensitive and selective sensor for the detection of luteolin, the linear range was 0–100 µM and the detection limit was 30 nmol/L. In practical sample analysis, the recovery rate and the RSD were 94.96 %–107 % and 0.54 %–3.52 %, respectively. In addition, CPDs-3 was used to construct a fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of water in the range of 0 % to 90 %. Moreover, as a potential nano-fluorescent probe, CPDs-2 showed low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility in cancer cell imaging research and plant seed growth processes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial with a size of less than 10 nm and possess many exceptional properties, such as environmental friendliness, excellent biocompatibility, tunable photoluminescence, high quantum yield, and unique physicochemical properties. With the deepening of research, researchers have proposed different definitions of the concept of CDs. CDs can be divided into carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) based on their distinct structures and properties [1–3]. For CPDs, their luminescence properties and colors can be significantly tuned through strategic approaches such as optimizing polymer design, selecting appropriate precursors, modulating reaction process, and incorporating heteroatom doping. During the formation process of CPDs, the precursors typically undergo condensation or polymerization, cross-linking, and further carbonization, ultimately yielding form quantum dot microstructures with highly cross-linked and carbonized nuclei inside and polymer structures outside [4,5].

The types of precursors have a significant impact on enhancing fluorescence intensity and adjusting the color of photoluminescence of CPDs. A wide range of precursors such as grass, strawberry powder, egg white, a large number of small molecules and some polymers (polysaccharides), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), polyethyleneimine (PEI), polyethylene glycol (PEG), polyacrylamide (PAM)) have been extensively used to synthesize CPDs [6–18]. Among these reports, those with multicolor properties exhibit particularly promising application potential due to their versatility in displaying multiple colors. For instance, Meng and colleagues achieved multicolor room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) CPDs ranging from green to orange via thermally driven amorphous topological phase transitions, and demonstrating not only color diversity but also advanced information encryption capabilities through a multi-stage application process [19]. Yang et al. successfully developed phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped CPDs with multicolor RTP including the blue color by microwave-assisted chemical reactions under different reaction times. Because of their excellent optical properties, the four CPDs have been successfully applied in preliminary anticounterfeiting paintings [20]. Lu’s group prepared multicolor fluorescent CPDs using a single unconjugated precursor (glucose). The emission wavelength of CPDs can be tuned in the range of 440–625 nm by controlling the particle size and degree of graphitization of the synthesized CPDs. More excitingly, the prepared CPDs can be used to construct LEDs of varying colors including WLEDs [21]. They also reported the synthesis of red and multicolor-emitting chiral-CPDs using the common precursors, L-/D-tryptophan and o-phenylenediamine. Importantly, circularly polarized white-emitting CPDs were fabricated for the first time by tuning the mixing ratio of the three colored Chiral-CPDs in a gel [22]. Lv’s group used concentrated hydrochloric acid to regulate the fluorescence of B, N-CPDs via a solvothermal reaction. The fluorescence of the B, N-CPDs was successfully adjusted from 533 nm to 580 nm by adding different volumes of concentrated hydrochloric acid to the precursor solution. The pH response property and poor water solubility of B,N-CPDs-1000 were utilized for pH sensing and trace water detection [23]. Xu’s group reported full-color luminescent CPDs which could target lipid by changing the reaction precursors. Fully purified CPDs exhibited fluorescence ranging from blue to red, and the results showed that their PL red-shift was directly correlated with the quantum size and surface state of the CPDs [24]. Despite these remarkable achievements, significant challenges remain in synthesizing multicolor CPDs with solid-state luminescence. Therefore, it is of great significance to obtain CPDs with multicolor and solid-state fluorescence properties simultaneously for their applications.

In this study, multicolor solid-state CPDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal condensation crosslinking carbonization (HCCC) strategy using a novel precursor, pyromellitic dianhydride (PMDA) and ethylenediamine (EDA). The as-prepared multicolor CPDs exhibited excellent water solubility, good solid-state PL properties, high photostability, size uniformity, and low cytotoxicity. Owing to its high fluorescence, the newly synthesized CPDs were applied effectively in LED, fluorescent flexible composite films, bioimaging, and as a fluorescence platform for sensitive and selective determination of luteolin and water.

Pyromellitic dianhydride (PMDA), quinine sulfate, genistein, quercetin, rutin, luteolin, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), DMSO, ethanol, methanol, acetonitrile, and acetone were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine (EDA) was from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). PVA powder was obtained from Beijing Innochem Science & Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). All metal salts and anion including CdCl2, MgCl2, ZnCl2, CrCl3, AlCl3, CaCl2, BaCl2, NiCl2, PbCl2, MnCl2, HgCl2, CuCl2, CoCl2, FeCl3, NaNO3, Na2CO3, Na2S2O3, NaNO2, NaCl, NaI, NaBr, CH3COONa and Na2S were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagents Factory (Beijing, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used directly without further purification. Ultrapure water was used in all experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of CPDs were observed on a JEOL-JEM 2100F apparatus at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained with a ThermoFisher ESCALAB Xi+ electron spectrometer. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (500–4000 cm−1) were recorded on an FTIR-650 spectrophotometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with an Analytikjena SPECORD 210 plus UV–vis spectrometer. Fluorescence measurements were conducted with a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. The thickness of the four-way and double-way quartz cuvette is 1 cm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted by the Bruker D8 FOCUS X at a 5°/min sweep rate in the 5–80°. The fluorescence lifetime was recorded by a Horiba Master-80 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (excitation wavelength of 405 nm). The NMR spectra were carried out with a Bruker ASCEND 500 spectrometer, and DMSO‑d6 was used as the solvent. Beckman DelsaMax Pro was used to measure the Zeta potential. The cell imaging photos were taken with an Olympus FV 1200 confocal microscope using an excitation wavelength of 405 nm.

In the synthesis process of CPDs-1, 250 mg PMDA, 0.5 mL EDA, and 5 mL DMF were directly added to 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene inner lining and ultrasonically dispersed for 20 min. Then, the polytetrafluoroethylene inner lining high-pressure reactor containing the reactants was placed in an electric blast drying oven, and was heated constantly at 140 °C for 4 h. The product was naturally cooled to room temperature, and filtered with a 0.22 μm organic filter membrane to obtain the solution, the dark brown solution was treated with acetonitrile and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The precipitation was washed three times with acetonitrile, dissolved in appropriate water, and filtered through a 0.22 μm aqueous-based filter membrane to remove the residual large particles. Finally, the acquired filtrate solution was freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain CPDs-1 solid powder.

For the preparation of CPDs-2, 250 mg PMDA, 0.5 mL EDA, and 5 mL DMF were ultrasonic mixed in 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene inner lining for 20 min, and hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 4 h. The subsequent treatment process was the same as the CPDs-1, and the final product was freeze-dried to obtain CPDs-2 solid powder. For the preparation of CPDs-3 and CPDs-4, the amount of PMDA was adjusted to 350 mg and 400 mg, respectively, other conditions were the same as the preparation conditions of CPDs-2.

Unblemished mung beans and black beans of similar size were selected carefully to conduct subsequent bean sprouting experiments. The selected beans were firstly soaked in pure water for one night and unstretched beans were discarded again. All soaked beans that meet the requirements were divided equally into three groups (named as a control group, low concentration test group, and high concentration test group), which were cultured with 250 mL pure water, 300 μg/mL and 500 μg/mL CPDs-2 solution in dark light, respectively. The culture solution was changed once every day. Bioimaging research was performed on the seventh day.

Breast cancer cells (MCF-7) were used to evaluate biotoxicity and further imaging studies. MCF-7 cells were preferentially cultured in 96-well plates for 24 h. Subsequently, the medium was replaced with CPDs-2 solution with different concentrations (50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, and 500 μg/mL) and the culture was continued for 24 h. Finally, 10 μL of MTT reagent (5.0 mg/mL) was added and the culture was continued for 4 h. The culture solution was sucked, DMSO was added and further shaken for 15 min, then A570 was measured using a microplate reader. The cell viability value was calculated using A570 (test group)/A570 (control group), where the test and control test represent the MCF-7 cells cultured in a medium with and without CPDs-2, respectively. For imaging experiments, MCF-7 cells were firstly incubated in a confocal specialized dish in a 37 °C incubator for 24 h, next the adherent cells were washed three times with PBS buffer, then those cells were further incubated with CPDs-2 solution (300 μg/mL) for 1 h. After washing three times with PBS buffer, the cells were subjected to imaging research (excitation wavelength λex = 405 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PVA fluorescence film and construction of LED chip
ce:section.ce:para: 1 g of PVA powder was added to 10 mL ultrapure water and stirred thoroughly at 100 °C to dissolve completely. 1.5 mL of 100 mg/mL CPDs solution was added in 1.5 mL of PVA glue solution and mixed thoroughly until there was no bubbles. The mixed glue solution was spread on a coverslip and air-dried naturally for 12 h to get a flexible PVA film containing high concentration of CPDs. On the other hand, the mixed glue solution was dripped on the 395 nm chip and dried naturally to achieve the LED device. In the above experiment, 10 mg/mL CPDs solution was used to displace 100 mg/mL CPDs solution, the as-obtained mixture solution could be applied to construct a flexible PVA film containing low concentration of CPDs and the corresponding LED device

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of luteolin based on CPDs-2 probe
ce:section.ce:para: CPDs-2 was dissolved into ultrapure water to obtain a working solution with a concentration of 30 μg/mL and stored in a refrigerator for future use. 10 μL of luteolin working solution with different concentrations was added into 2 mL of CPDs-2 working solution and reacted for 2 min, then emission spectra were measured at the excitation wavelength of 410 nm. In the interference experiments, 10 μL of interfering substance (including metal ions, anions, and small molecules) solution with 0.01 mol/L was added to 2 mL of the CPDs-2 working solution, and the fluorescence intensity was measured after reacting for 2 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of luteolin in practical samples
ce:section.ce:para: 1000 g of grinded peanut shell powder are dissolved in 1000 mL of 0.01 M NaOH solution, followed by soaking for 6 h under a sealed container at room temperature, the obtained mixture solution was centrifugated at 5000 rpm/min for 10 min. Subsequently, supernatant is filtered using a 0.22 μm filter head to remove insoluble components. Then 10 μL of the filtrate and 10 μL of standard solution is added into 2 mL of CPDs-2 solution, and the fluorescence intensity of the mixtures at 484 nm was measured at an excitation wavelength of 410 nm after 2 min of incubation.

For comparison, the samples were also analyzed by UV–vis spectroscopy in this study. Different concentrations of 20 µL of luteolin standard solution were added into 2.0 mL H2O medium, and the absorption spectra were scanned in the wavelength range of 250 nm to 800 nm, and the standard curves were plotted between the absorbance at the maximal absorption wavelength and the concentration of luteolin. In recovery experiments, 10 μL of the filtrate and 10 μL of standard solution is added into 2 mL of CPDs-2 solution, and the fluorescence intensity of the mixtures at 484 nm was measured at an excitation wavelength of 410 nm after 2 min of incubation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of water content in DMSO
ce:section.ce:para: CPDs-3 was dissolved into DMSO solvent to obtain a working solution with a concentration of 2 mg/mL. Then, 200 μL of the above solution was poured into 2 mL of the centrifuge tube, and 1800 μL of DMSO solution containing different amounts of water was further added and mixed completely, then the emission spectra were measured under an excitation wavelength of 410 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of multicolor solid-state CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation route of multicolor CPDs was shown in Scheme 1 . Four varieties of CPDs with distinct solid-state fluorescence were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal approach under varying reaction conditions by employing PMDA and EDA as the monomers (the optimization conditions of CPDs were presented in Table S1). The quantum yields of CPDs-1, CPDs-2, CPDs-3, and CPDs-4 were calculated as 29.50 %, 51.56 %, 45.84 %, and 49.72 % in aqueous medium, respectively. The formation process of CPDs went through three stages. Firstly, in non-protonic solvent DMF, the amine group on EDA conducts the nucleophilic attack to the carbonyl carbon atom on the anhydride, leading to the opening of the anhydride rings on both sides of PMDA to form the intermediate with the amide bond and free carboxyl group. In the case of high temperature, high pressure and an excess of EDA, intermediate and EDA may further react through two pathways. In reaction pathway (1), the amide group further condenses with the carboxyl group on the intermediate to form an imide derivative, and the amino group at the end of the imide derivative will further reacts with free PMDA, forming long chain polymer I. In reaction pathway (2), the carboxyl group on the intermediate will react with the amine group on free EDA, while the amine group at the end of the amide chain on the intermediate will further undergo nucleophilic reaction with free PMDA, as a result, a long chain polymer II is formed. During the condensation reaction at this stage, pathways (1) and (2) can simultaneously occur and there is a competitive relationship. The reaction conditions including the ratio of raw materials and hydrothermal conditions can affect this competitive relationship to a certain extent. In the second stage, the long polymer chain will continuously coil to form the polymer network structure. Meanwhile, these carbon structures will cause slight carbonization. In the third stage, the polymer structures gradually diminish with the deepening of the dehydration and carbonization degree, finally, a core–shell quasi-crystalline carbon core structure wrapped by the polymer framework was formed.

XPS analysis revealed the elemental composition and surface structures of four CPDs. As depicted in Fig. 1 , these CPDs primarily contained three elements: carbon (∼285 eV), nitrogen (∼400 eV), and oxygen (∼531 eV). In contrast, the oxygen content of 25.48 % in CPDs-1 was higher than that of CPDs-4 (20.33 %). This implied that the reaction pathway (1) was dominant in the condensation and nucleation process of CPDs-1. For the other three CPDs, reaction pathway (2) tended to proceed with increasing PMDA dosage under the same hydrothermal condition resulting in the decrease in oxygen content in the CPDs. Delving into the high-resolution C1s spectra, it was deconvolved into four distinct peaks centered at 284.7, 285.9, 287.0, and 287.7 eV, corresponding to C–C/C=C, C–N, C–O, and C=O bonds, respectively [25]. Among these peaks, the C–C/C=C bonds occupied a larger proportion, indicating more sp2 carbon structure within the CPDs [26]. Similarly, the high-resolution N1s spectrum revealed two prominent peaks at 399.5 and 401.2 eV, attributing to amide N and N–H groups, respectively [27,28]. It confirmed the presence of an N-doped graphitic carbon skeleton in the CPDs [29]. According to the specific peak data (Table S2), the content of amide N gradually increased from CPDs-1 to CPDs-4, further indicating that the condensation process in the formation of CPDs-4 inclined to occur towards the reaction pathway (2). These results were completely consistent with the results obtained from FTIR. Additionally, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. S1) deconvolved into two distinct peaks centered at 531.7, 53 3.4 eV, corresponding to C=O and C–O bonds [30–32].

Fig. 2 A presented the TEM images of four CPDs and their corresponding size distributions. These CPDs particles displayed a quasi-spherical morphology with large interparticle spacing and there was no accumulation. HRTEM images of four CPDs showed distinct lattice fringes, signifying the presence of large conjugated structures within the carbon cores of CPDs. Notably, the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponded to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon, further corroborating this structural feature [33,34]. According to the statistics of 100 nanoparticles, the average particle diameters of CPDs-1, CPDs-2, CPDs-3, and CPDs-4 were estimated as 1.83 nm, 1.91 nm, 1.93 nm, and 2.36 nm, respectively. Narrow half-peak width of the gaussian curve on size distribution histogram demonstrated that four CPDs have uniform particle size.

The XRD spectra in Fig. 2B showed that all four CPDs exhibited a prominent broad diffraction peak centered at approximately 21.4–22.4° (2θ). This characteristic peak was assigned precisely with the (002) crystal plane of the sp2 graphite structure, implying the existence of a high conjugated sp2 configuration within these CPDs [29,35,36]. The FTIR spectra in Fig. 2C revealed unique signatures of PMDA with characteristic peaks at 1850 and 1776 cm−1 corresponding to the coupled vibrational modes of the two carbonyl groups (C=O) within the anhydride molecule. The peak at 1228 cm−1 was attributed to the C–O stretching vibration specific to cyclic anhydrides. In contrast, the broadband from 3200 to 3500 cm−1 in FTIR spectra of CPDs was assigned to N–H and O–H stretching vibrations [37–39]. Peaks emerging at 3050, 2971, and 2890 cm−1 corresponded to =C–H, C–C, and C–H stretching vibrations, respectively [37,39,40]. The sharp peak at 1655 cm−1 signified C=O stretching vibration within an amide structure [41–43]. While the peak at 1535 cm−1 corresponded to N–H bending vibration [43]. Furthermore, the peak at 1380 cm−1 arose from C–N stretching vibration [38,43]. Compared with the FTIR spectra of PMDA, the absence of characteristic peaks at 1850/1776 cm−1 and 1228 cm−1 confirmed that the anhydride has reacted with EDA to convert into amide or imide structures. These data indicated that CPDs surface contains amide groups, C=C, O–H, and N–H functional groups.

The precise structure of CPDs was further investigated by hydrogen nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (H-NMR). According to the H-NMR spectra of PMDA and four CPDs in Fig. 2D, chemical shift peaks of 2.0–3.0 ppm, 3.0–3.7 ppm and 7.9–8.3 ppm were attributed to –CH2– moieties, the terminal-NH2 groups and the aryl ring hydrogens, respectively. The small peaks at approximately 5.57 ppm originated from the hydrogen on amide N. Furthermore, the small peaks around 8.6 ppm were associated with the hydrogen of –COOH functional group. These data confirmed the existence of the amide bonds, aromatic sp2 carbon, –CH2– units, and N–H groups within the CPDs structure [26,44,45]. The histogram depicted in Fig. 2E displayed that the surfaces of the as-synthesized CPDs were negatively charged, which was mainly caused by the carboxyl groups on the surface of CPDs. The Zeta potentials of CPDs-1, CPDs-2, CPDs-3 and CPDs-4 were measured as −13.5, −21.32, −21.88 and −23.02 mV, respectively. Gradual increased Zeta potential further verified that the condensation process of CPDs was more inclined toward the reaction pathway (2) while PMDA dosage increased, which is consistent with XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability of CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: The solubility and luminescent properties of CPDs in various solvents were investigated in detail. From Fig. 3 A, it was observed that CPDs-1 exhibited good solubility in DMSO and aqueous solution, slight solubility in methanol and ethanol, and insolubility in DMF and acetone. The dispersion solutions of CPDs-1 in several solvents emitted cyan fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation. The other three CPDs were insoluble in acetone, somewhat soluble in methanol and ethanol, and completely soluble in DMSO, water and DMF. Furthermore, their dispersion solutions mainly emitted green, yellow or yellow-green fluorescence. Fig. 3B showed the optical photographs of four CPDs powders under sunlight and 365 nm UV light. it could be seen that CPDs-1, CPDs-2, CPDs-3, and CPDs-4 exhibited fluorescent yellow, yellow, brownish-yellow, and brown color under sunlight, and they displayed cyan, bright yellow, yellow and brownish-yellow fluorescence under UV light. It indicated that the as-prepared CPDs have solid-state fluorescence characteristics, and the fluorescence color of the solid was completely different from the solution state. This solid-state fluorescence property has the potential for fluorescence dyeing and the construction of LED devices.

The UV–vis absorption spectra and the corresponding excitation and emission spectra of the four CPDs aqueous solutions were depicted in Fig. 3C. The shoulder peak at 285 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra originated from the π–π* transition of the C=C/C=N bonds, indicating that partial N was successfully doped into the carbon nucleus during carbonization nucleation. In addition, a significant absorption peak located at 395 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra of the other three CPDs (CPDs-2, CPDs-3, and CPDs-4) was assigned to the n–π* transition of the C=N/C=O bonds. More importantly, this absorption peak position was relatively coincident with the optimal excitation wavelengths of the three CPDs (∼400 nm), suggesting that this characteristic peak has a certain contribution to the fluorescence emission of these three CPDs [46]. However, the absence of the peak at 395 nm in UV–vis spectra of CPDs-1 indicated that there was less amide N in the structure of CPDs-1, which was consistent with the XPS and HNMR results. It is considered that the fluorescence emission of CPDs-1 may originate from the quantum size effect or surface functional groups due to incomplete carbonization. Meanwhile, the band gap energy of CPDs was calculated as 1.939–1.991 eV according to the solid UV data of CPDs (see Fig. S2), which was consistent with the literature reports [47].

The fluorescence emission spectra of the four CPDs in several solvents under different excitation wavelengths were shown in Fig. S3. For CPDs-2, CPDs-3, and CPDs-4, two emission peaks were observed at ∼400 nm and ∼500 nm when the excitation wavelength λex < 360 nm, then the peak at ∼400 nm disappeared and the emission peak position of ∼500 nm kept constant when λex > 360. This excitation-independent fluorescence emission behavior suggested that these CPDs have homogeneous size and surface state. However, the fluorescence emission characteristics of CPDs-1 were different from those of the other three. The optimal emission peaks in several solvents were located at 410–430 nm, and the peak positions have a slight red shift with the change of the excitation wavelength, which is possibly attributed to surface state differences from incomplete carbonization.

It is well known that stability plays a key role in the potential applications of nanomaterials. As displayed in Fig. S4, the four CPDs still maintained stable optical performance under the conditions of pH 3–11, UV irradiation for 120 min, 1.0 mol/L high salt environment, and even storage for up to 10 days. it showed that CPDs possessed good stability and could be used in physiological environments or other extreme conditions.

Considering the good stability and optical properties of CPDs, the potential applications of CPDs in fluorescent films and LED devices were investigated. CPDs/PVA films containing 5 mg/mL and 50 mg/mL CPDs and the corresponding LED devices were prepared (see Fig. 3D). It was found that the fluorescence films and LED devices displayed different fluorescence colors at lower and higher CPDs contents. When CPDs content was 5 mg/mL, CPDs-1/PVA, CPDs-2/PVA, CPDs-3/PVA, and CPDs-4/PVA composite films and the corresponding LED devices presented blue, light yellow, and green fluorescence, respectively. When CPDs content was 50 mg/mL, the fluorescent color of CPDs/PVA composite films and LED devices were different from that of low content of CPDs, and mainly exhibited fluorescent green and orange fluorescence. This implies that it is possible to achieve the modulation of the color of composite film and LED light emission by adjusting the content of CPDs, which provides an opportunity for the diversified modulation of the light source of LED light-emitting devices.

CPDs-2 was selected as a fluorescent probe to investigate its application in fluorescence sensing. The response capacity of CPDs-2 towards metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Zn2+), anions (NO2 −, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, NO3 −, Cl−, I−, Br−, CH3COO−, S2−), and small molecules (rutin, genistein, quercetin and luteolin) was priorly explored. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, it was obvious that luteolin could bring about a significant fluorescence quenching of CPDs-2, and the corresponding relative fluorescence ratio (F0/F) reached 2.4, but there was no apparent fluorescence change in the presence of other metal ions, anions, and small molecules. This suggests that CPDs-2 has high selectivity towards luteolin which can be used for the determination of luteolin. From Fig. S5, it was further found that the fluorescence of the system decreased rapidly and remained stable within the initial 10 min. To get fast and stable response signals, 2 min was used as the response time in the subsequent detection process. As shown in Fig. 4B and C, the fluorescence of CPDs-2 gradually decreased with the addition of luteolin, and there was good linear relationship between the decreased fluorescence values (F0-F) and the concentration of luteolin in the range of 0–100 µM. And the limit of detection was estimated to be 30 nM according to the 3-fold signal-to-noise ratio rule.

CPDs-2 was further applied to the determination of luteolin in real samples. Using the spiked method, we determined the content and recovery rate of luteolin in water samples (as tested) and peanut shell extraction. The results showed that luteolin was not detected in water samples, with a spiked recovery rate ranging from 94.96 % to 107 %, accompanied by relative standard deviations between 0.54 % and 0.96 %. For peanut shell extraction, the content of luteolin was estimated as 0.26 g in per 1 kg peanut shell, the spiked recovery rate was found to be 96.96 %-103.6 % with RSD values of 0.88 %-2.04 %. To further validate the reliability of our proposed method, UV–vis spectroscopy was used to measure luteolin content and recovery rates in water samples and peanut shell extraction (the corresponding standard curve was depicted in Fig. S6). As shown in Table 1 , the results obtained by both methods were not significantly different, demonstrating good agreement between them. These findings indicate that the proposed method is reliable and feasible for analyzing luteolin in real samples.

It is well known that the fluorescence quench mechanism generally involves dynamic quenching, static quenching, internal filtering effect, and energy resonance transfer. As displayed in Fig. 4D and E, both the fluorescence lifetime curves of CPDs-2 before and after adding luteolin conformed to the second-order fitting mode, and the average fluorescence lifetimes were 3.83 ns and 3.85 ns, respectively. Unchanged lifetime indicated that the fluorescence quenching of CPDs-2 by luteolin belongs to the static quenching type. As seen in Fig. S7, there was no obvious overlap between UV–vis spectrum of luteolin and the emission spectrum of CPDs-2, manifesting that the fluorescence quenching mechanism does not involve energy resonance transfer. Furthermore, it was observed that the Stern-Volmer constants decreased with increasing temperature (Fig. 4F). This further verified that the fluorescence quenching process obeys the static quenching mechanism.

More surprisingly, we further discovered that the addition of water into CPDs-3 DMSO solution could cause the fluorescence variation of CPDs-3. Based on this finding, we applied CPDs-3 to construct a fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of water. As shown in Fig. 4G and H, the fluorescence intensity of CPDs-3 gradually increased with the increase of water content from 0–90 % accompanied by a blue shift of 16 nm of the emission peak. Meanwhile, the Fluorescence color also varied from green to blue, and the chromaticity coordinates shifted from green region (0.2506, 0.4561) to blue region (0.1870, 0.3777) in CIE diagram. The fluorescence intensity has a linear relationship with the water content in the range of 0–90 % (Fig. 4I). Linear fitting equation is F0 – F = −1.427C + 11.60 with R2 = 0.9996.

CPDs-2 was further selected as a nano-fluorescent probe to investigate its application in cell imaging and plant seed growth. The cytotoxicity of CPDs-2 to MCF-7 cells was evaluated using an MTT assay. The viability of MCF-7 cells was as high as 97 % even at a concentration of CPDs-2 up to 500 μg/mL after incubating with CPDs-2 solution for 24 h (Fig. 5 A), which indicated that the cytotoxicity of CPDs-2 was extremely low. Meanwhile, we employed CPDs-2 solutions with different concentrations (0, 300, and 500 mg/L) as a culture medium to cultivate black bean and mung bean seeds. After incubating for 7 days, both black bean and mung bean seeds grew well, and the thickness and length of bean sprouts cultured by CPDs-2 medium had no obvious difference with the control group (Fig. 5B and C). This confirmed that the presence of CPDs-2 did not affect the germination and growth of the seeds, exhibiting good biocompatibility.

In the cell imaging research, it was observed that MCF-7 cells exhibited good cellular morphology after incubating with CPDs-2 solution for 1 h, and apparent fluorescence was displayed in the cytoplasmic region rather than the nucleus region (Fig. 5D), suggesting that CPDs-2 mainly enter the cytoplasmic region. Moreover, confocal analysis (Fig. 5E and F) of the stem slices of black bean sprouts showed that the cell morphology of CPDs-2 incubation group was consistent with the control group. In contrast, there was no fluorescence in the plant cells of the control group, but the vascular system region of CPDs-2 incubation group presented significant fluorescence. This also verified that CPDs-2 nanoprobe easily entered into the vascular system of plant cells in the process of incubating the sprouts. All these results demonstrated that CPDs-2 has the advantages of low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, which is a potential nano-fluorescent probe in bioimaging fields.

In summary, this work demonstrates that four CPDs with different solid-state fluorescence can be successfully synthesized from PMDA and EDA as the monomer molecules by hydrothermal condensation cross-linking carbonization (HCCC) technique. The obtained CPDs-1, CPDs-2, CPDs-3, and CPDs-4 possessed the quantum yields of 29.50 %, 51.56 %, 45.84 %, and 49.72 % in aqueous medium, respectively. TEM results displayed that four kind of CPDs had a uniform particle size estimated as 1.83 nm, 1.91 nm, 1.93 nm, and 2.36 nm, respectively. Structure analysis suggested that CPDs contained amide bonds, aromatic sp2 carbon, –CH2– units, and N–H groups. Moreover, it also confirmed that the condensation process in the formation of CPDs is more inclined to go on toward the reaction pathway (2) with increasing the PMDA dosage. These CPDs not only exhibited good fluorescence properties but also possessed excellent optical stability even under some extreme conditions. Results showed that four CPDs could construct CPDs/PVA fluorescence composite films and LED devices, and the color of composite film and LED light emission could be adjusted by modulating content of CPDs. In sensing research, CPDs-2 was employed as nanoprobe for sensitive and selective the detection of luteolin, which exhibited a wide linear range of 0–100 μM with a low limit of detection, as low as 30 nM. The approach provides a new process for the determination of luteolin in real samples with the recoveries between 94.96 % and 107 % and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) between 0.54 % and 3.52 %. Furthermore, CPDs-2 showed low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility in cancer cell imaging research and plant seed growth processes. CPDs-3 can be successfully constructed a fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of water in the range of 0–90 %.

In this research, four CPDs with different solid-state fluorescence were successfully synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal condensation cross-linking carbonization (HCCC) approach employing pyromellitic dianhydride (PMDA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as the polymerization monomers. It was proposed that there are two competing reaction pathways in the formation stage of polymers, and regulation of reaction conditions can affect this competitive relationship to acquire CPDs with different solid-state fluorescence. The morphology and surface structure of four kinds of CPDs were characterized in detail using several characterization techniques. These four CPDs not only possessed small particle sizes of 1.83–2.36 nm, but also exhibited excellent fluorescence stability. Results displayed that CPDs-1, CPDs-2, CPDs-3, and CPDs-4 solid powders exhibited fluorescent yellow, yellow, brownish-yellow, and brown fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp, which could fabricate fluorescent films and apply in LED devices. Interestingly, it was noticed that the modulation of the content of CPDs in CPDs/PVA composite films or the surface of LED chips could greatly adjust the color of composite film and LED light emission. In sensing studies, CPDs-2 could serve as fluorescence probe to construct a sensitive and selective sensor for the detection of luteolin, the linear range was 0–100 µM and the detection limit was 30 nmol/L. In practical sample analysis, the recovery rate and the RSD were 94.96 %–107 % and 0.54 %–3.52 %, respectively. In addition, CPDs-3 was used to construct a fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of water in the range of 0 %–90 %. Moreover, as a potential nano-fluorescent probe, CPDs-2 showed low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility in cancer cell imaging research and plant seed growth processes.
Pengyi Ma: Writing – original draft. Jinping Song: Writing – review & editing. Shutao Li: Data curation. Xiaoting Guo: Investigation. Jianhua Xue: Data curation. Qi Ma: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113066.
